character_creation.philosophy: This is not a character builder. It is an intake interview. The player never sees a stat sheet during creation. They answer questions and the world places them accordingly. Only after creation do they see what they're working with.
character_creation.tone: Every question carries the world's voice: tired, unsentimental, bureaucratic. No heroic framing. No 'what is your destiny.' Just: who are you, where did you come from, and what can you do.

options.text: Cold north — forests, snow seasons, short summers
Temperate middle — farmland, river valleys, seasonal variation
Dry interior — plateau, scrub, dust and heat
Coastal — salt air, fishing, trade routes
Wetland/moor — fen, bog, dark forest, rain
Island/maritime — the sea was always visible

questions.prompt: How many people lived where you grew up?

options.text: Fewer than fifty — isolated farmstead, hamlet, shepherd's community
A few hundred — village with a market day
A few thousand — town with a garrison and a temple
A city — walls, districts, a lord's keep or a council hall
No fixed home — you moved

questions.mechanical_effect: Starting social familiarity. City: urban systems, guild structures, bureaucracy navigation. Rural: terrain, weather, animal behavior, seasonal labor. Nomadic: roads, reading strangers quickly.

options.text: Two parents, both living (or were, when you left)
One parent — the other dead, absent, or unknown
Extended family — grandparents, aunts, older siblings
An institution — temple, guild hall, army camp
Nobody in particular — you were passed around or raised yourself

questions.mechanical_effect: Starting relationship capital. Institutional upbringing: structure skills + specific faction affiliation. Nobody in particular: higher baseline streetwise and stealth, lower NPC trust at game start.

questions.prompt: What did the person who raised you do for a living?

options.text: Farmed land (owned or tenant)
Worked a trade (blacksmith, carpenter, tanner, cooper, etc.)
Soldiered (regular army, militia, or mercenary)
Kept a business (inn, shop, mill)
Served a lord or institution (steward, priest, clerk)
Died before you could know

questions.mechanical_effect: First skill inheritance. Tradesperson: Apprentice-level in that trade. Soldier: basic weapon familiarity. Clergy/steward: literacy and religious/administrative lore.

options.text: Conscripted or pressed into service
Ran from something (debt, crime, a person)
Followed someone (lover, friend, sibling, mentor)
Sent away (apprenticeship, service, disgrace)
The place you were died (burned, starved, taken)
You chose it — you wanted something different
You never left; you're still there
options.availability: Only available for certain starting zones

questions.mechanical_effect: Creates a background thread — a debt, a warrant, a relationship, a loss — that exists in the world and may surface during play.

options.text: Yes, the faith I was raised in
options.note: Present list based on continent of origin

options.text: Yes, but I found it myself — not the one I was born to
I observe the forms but believe nothing
I believe in nothing and make no secret of it
I don't think about it

questions.mechanical_effect: Starting religious affiliation. Active believers get access to community support systems (temple will feed/shelter). Atheism creates friction in theocratic zones.

questions.prompt: Have you ever had to hurt someone?

options.text: Yes, in proper military service
Yes, but not military — a brawl, a road, something personal
Yes, and I don't want to talk about what happened
No, but I've been hurt
No, never

questions.mechanical_effect: Combat history flag. Military: basic formation fighting, weapon maintenance, chain of command. Brawl: unarmed and dirty fighting basics, no formal training. Never fought: no combat skills but no trauma flag affecting early morale.

questions.prompt: What did you bring with you?

options.text: The clothes I'm wearing and not much else
Basic tools of a trade
A weapon I know how to use (somewhat)
A small amount of coin — enough for a few days
A horse or pack animal
options.note: Significant advantage. Implies background with animals, knowledge of care, and something worth stealing.

options.text: A letter of introduction or a contract

questions.pass_description: Who you are. These questions feel personal. They map disposition, decision-making tendency, and hidden trait assignments. No right answers.

questions.prompt: A man on the road ahead of you falls. You can see he's injured, maybe robbed. It's getting dark and you're an hour from the nearest settlement. What do you do?

options.text: Help him and take the delay
Help him enough to get him moving, then part ways
Give him directions and keep going
Walk wide around him — could be a trap
Take stock of the situation before deciding anything

questions.mechanical_effect: Maps to empathy/compassion trait, risk assessment tendency, social disposition. Not a morality test — walking wide is a reasonable response in a dangerous world.

questions.prompt: You've been three days without a proper meal. Someone in the market drops a purse and doesn't notice. What do you do?

options.text: Take it
Take it but follow them to make sure they're not destitute
Leave it and hope someone honest finds it
Call after them
Turn it in to whoever passes for authority here

questions.mechanical_effect: Maps to desperation threshold, moral flexibility, relationship with authority. Affects starting reputation in the economy simulation.

questions.prompt: The work you've been doing for the past month is honest, legal, and soul-destroying. The pay is fair. A stranger offers you something more interesting but considerably more dangerous. What matters most to you?

options.text: Security
Coin
Something to do that means something
Staying alive
It depends entirely on what the dangerous thing is

questions.mechanical_effect: Maps to risk tolerance, motivation archetype. Security: better morale stability. Coin: mercantile instinct bonuses. Meaning: higher loyalty ceiling with factions but higher betrayal sensitivity.

questions.prompt: Someone you work with — not a friend, just a colleague — is skimming from the common stores. Not enough to really hurt anyone. Do you say something?

options.text: Yes, to them directly
Yes, to whoever's in charge
No — it's not my business
No — I'd probably do the same in their position
It depends on whether I like them

questions.mechanical_effect: Maps to loyalty type, institutional vs personal ethics. Affects how faction reputation accrues.

questions.prompt: How would you describe yourself physically?

options.text: Lean and quick
Average, nothing remarkable
Heavy and strong
Tall
Short and wiry

questions.mechanical_effect: Seeds base physical stats. Starting points, not ceilings.

options.text: Young — sixteen to twenty
options.notes: Maximum skill ceiling, slowest current skills, high physical recovery, low social credibility

options.text: Early prime — twenty to thirty
Mid-life — thirty to forty-five
options.notes: More skills partially trained, lower physical ceiling, existing relationship threads

options.text: Older — forty-five to sixty
options.notes: Significantly developed skills, reduced physical peak, existing old enemies and old friends, taken seriously in negotiation

options.text: Old — sixty and above
options.notes: Extensive skills, major physical limitations, deep relationship web, extraordinary social authority in right contexts

questions.mechanical_effect: Age has genuine mechanical weight across the board.

questions.prompt: Is there anything else I should know about you?

options.text: I can read and write
options.notes: Rarer than modern players expect. Opens administrative, clerical, scholarly paths.

options.text: I know a second language
I have a condition — an injury, an illness, something chronic
I have a dependent — someone who relies on me somewhere
options.notes: Creates an off-screen obligation the economy simulation manages. If you stop providing, something happens.

options.text: I owe someone a significant debt
I have a name that means something in a particular place (reputation, positive or negative)
Nothing. I'm nobody.

result.background: Levy soldier recently released or deserted from a northern campaign
result.background_thread: The unit he left — whether honorably or not — is somewhere in the world and has opinions about him
result.notes: Flame faith gets him a cold welcome at the local temple if he asks for shelter (he observes but doesn't believe, and they can usually tell)

example_resolutions.label: Example B — The Former Temple Servant

result.background: Former temple servant — quietly left or was expelled. The reason is the hook.
result.background_thread: The dependent — a younger sibling or a child — is in a city somewhere, alive, needing periodic support
result.notes: The faith she found for herself is a minor one, not locally dominant, which creates friction in a Tidewarden-dominated port
